comment_text.sn35.80:6.2: “‘Nothing is worth insisting on” (sabbe dhammā nālaṁ abhinivesāyā) occurs several times (see especially MN 37:3.2 and note). “Insistence” (abinivesa) is a name for defilements that have become rigidly stuck, particularly the dogmatic insistence that one is correct and everyone else is wrong.
comment_text.sn35.80:6.5: Aññato means “as other”. Like parato (“as alien”), it is a term for not-self. | “All signs” are the features of experience that the mind hooks on to as desirable, as permanent, or as Self.

root_text.sn35.80:1.2: “atthi nu kho, bhante, eko dhammo yassa pahānā bhikkhuno avijjā pahīyati, vijjā uppajjatī”ti?
root_text.sn35.80:2.1: “Atthi kho, bhikkhu, eko dhammo yassa pahānā bhikkhuno avijjā pahīyati, vijjā uppajjatī”ti.
root_text.sn35.80:3.1: “Katamo pana, bhante, eko dhammo yassa pahānā bhikkhuno avijjā pahīyati, vijjā uppajjatī”ti?
root_text.sn35.80:4.1: “Avijjā kho, bhikkhu, eko dhammo yassa pahānā bhikkhuno avijjā pahīyati, vijjā uppajjatī”ti.
root_text.sn35.80:5.1: “Kathaṁ pana, bhante, jānato, kathaṁ passato avijjā pahīyati, vijjā uppajjatī”ti?
root_text.sn35.80:6.5: So sabbaṁ dhammaṁ abhijānāti, sabbaṁ dhammaṁ abhiññāya sabbaṁ dhammaṁ parijānāti, sabbaṁ dhammaṁ pariññāya sabbanimittāni aññato passati, cakkhuṁ aññato passati, rūpe …
root_text.sn35.80:6.8: yampidaṁ cakkhusamphassapaccayā uppajjati vedayitaṁ sukhaṁ vā dukkhaṁ vā adukkhamasukhaṁ vā tampi aññato passati …pe…
root_text.sn35.80:6.12: yampidaṁ manosamphassapaccayā uppajjati vedayitaṁ sukhaṁ vā dukkhaṁ vā adukkhamasukhaṁ vā tampi aññato passati.
root_text.sn35.80:6.13: Evaṁ kho, bhikkhu, jānato evaṁ passato bhikkhuno avijjā pahīyati, vijjā uppajjatī”ti.

translation_text.sn35.80:1.1: Then a mendicant went up to the Buddha … and asked him,
translation_text.sn35.80:1.2: “Sir, is there one thing such that by giving it up a mendicant gives up ignorance and gives rise to knowledge?”
translation_text.sn35.80:4.1: “Ignorance is one thing such that by giving it up a mendicant gives up ignorance and gives rise to knowledge.”
translation_text.sn35.80:5.1: “But how does a mendicant know and see so as to give up ignorance and give rise to knowledge?”
translation_text.sn35.80:6.3: When a mendicant has heard that
translation_text.sn35.80:6.5: they directly know all things. Directly knowing all things, they completely understand all things. Completely understanding all things, they see all signs as other. They see the eye, sights,
translation_text.sn35.80:6.8: And they also see the pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on eye contact as other. …
translation_text.sn35.80:6.12: And they also see the pleasant, painful, or neutral feeling that arises dependent on mind contact as other.
translation_text.sn35.80:6.13: That’s how a mendicant knows and sees so as to give up ignorance and give rise to knowledge.”
